past_medical_history: No prior hospitalizations before current illness
Childhood history unremarkable per father's report

medications.indication: Empiric treatment for suspected peptic ulcer disease

family_history: Mother deceased when patient was 12 years old (details unclear, reported as sudden illness)
Father healthy, no psychiatric or neurological conditions
No family history of epilepsy, movement disorders, or autoimmune disease

social_history.occupation: Secondary school student, stopped attending 4 months ago due to illness
social_history.living_situation: Lives with father and two younger siblings in a rural area; mother died 7 years ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Cooperative but speaks in a low, monotonous voice. Affect is constricted but not frankly depressed. She is irritable and intermittently tearful. No evidence of psychosis: no delusions, no hallucinations elicited on direct questioning. Thought process is linear and goal-directed. Cognition grossly intact — follows complex commands, recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Facial strength symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Severe diffuse muscle wasting most prominent in both lower extremities. Tone normal in upper extremities, mildly reduced in lower extremities. Give-way weakness in bilateral lower extremities — initial effort 3/5 followed by sudden collapse to 0/5, inconsistent with anatomical pattern. Upper extremity strength 4+/5 globally, likely related to deconditioning. During distraction testing, patient inadvertently demonstrates 4/5 hip flexion bilaterally when performing an upper extremity task. Hoover sign positive bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports complete loss of sensation to light touch and pinprick below the waist in a non-dermatomal distribution. Vibration and proprioception intact in all extremities. Sensory examination findings are inconsistent — she withdraws to painful stimuli applied to the feet when distracted.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 1+ bilaterally (possibly reduced due to muscle wasting). Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally in upper extremities. Heel-to-shin testing not reliably performed due to reported leg weakness.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to stand or walk independently. When assisted to standing, demonstrates astasia-abasia pattern with dramatic swaying and lurching but does not fall. Catches herself using nearby objects in a manner requiring significant balance and coordination.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, patient had two episodes of rhythmic whole-body shaking lasting approximately 60 seconds each. During these episodes, eyes were tightly closed, she was unresponsive to verbal stimuli but resisted passive eye opening. Movements were asynchronous and waxed and waned in amplitude. No tongue biting, no incontinence, no post-ictal confusion — she returned to baseline conversation immediately after episodes ceased. Incessant retching observed throughout examination, but no vomitus produced.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive inability to walk, intractable vomiting, and episodes of whole-body shaking for 4 months
patient.history_present_illness: A 19-year-old right-handed female is admitted to the inpatient neurology service after transfer from the pediatric-medical ward for further evaluation of a 4-month progressive decline in function. Her illness began insidiously with persistent low back pain and headaches that did not respond to over-the-counter analgesics. She also reported a sensation of a 'living creature moving inside her abdomen,' which she found deeply distressing. Over the subsequent weeks, she developed intractable nausea with frequent retching and occasional vomiting, leading to marked decrease in oral intake and significant weight loss. Approximately two months into the illness, she began having difficulty walking, initially described as legs feeling heavy and weak, progressing over several weeks to complete inability to bear weight or ambulate. Her father reports that she has been essentially bed-bound for the past 6 weeks and has lost over 15 kilograms during the course of her illness. During the initial medical admission, she was found to be severely malnourished with a BMI of 14.8 and required nasogastric tube feeding. Multiple evaluations at regional hospitals prior to this admission included CT imaging of the abdomen and brain, as well as MRI of the spinal cord, all of which were reportedly normal. Extensive gastrointestinal workup including upper endoscopy was unremarkable. Laboratory screening for infectious, metabolic, and autoimmune causes was non-revealing. She was empirically treated for peptic ulcer disease with a proton pump inhibitor without improvement. In the past two weeks, she has also developed episodic whole-body shaking events that last 30 to 90 seconds, during which she appears unresponsive. These have been witnessed multiple times by nursing staff. She has no prior psychiatric history, although her father reports that in the months preceding the onset of symptoms, she appeared withdrawn, lost interest in schoolwork, expressed feelings of worthlessness, and had decreased energy. She was previously a high-performing student. Her mother died 7 years ago under circumstances that remain distressing to the family, and she assumed a caretaking role for her younger siblings. She denies any history of physical or sexual abuse when asked directly, but interview is limited. She is transferred for neurological assessment with a referral diagnosis of suspected early onset psychosis versus conversion disorder.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, well-formed and reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythm: Low-amplitude beta activity in frontal regions, within normal limits
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep spindles and vertex waves observed during drowsiness; K-complexes present in stage 2 sleep

findings.morphology: High-amplitude irregular artifact obscuring underlying EEG during two clinical episodes of whole-body shaking
findings.clinical_correlation: No epileptiform correlate identified during clinical events. The EEG remained obscured by muscle and movement artifact during the shaking episodes, but discernible underlying cerebral activity showed preserved posterior dominant rhythm without any ictal EEG pattern.

artifacts.location: Generalized, most prominent during clinical events
artifacts.description: Significant EMG artifact during two episodes of generalized shaking; underlying EEG readable through artifact and shows no epileptiform change

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: 3 minutes performed; no epileptiform discharges or clinical events provoked
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Normal EEG. Two clinical events consisting of generalized whole-body shaking were captured. No EEG correlate was identified during these events — the underlying cerebral activity showed preserved normal background without epileptiform discharges. These findings are consistent with non-epileptic events (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures).
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during clinical events partially obscured some channels, but sufficient channels remained readable to confirm absence of ictal EEG pattern.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical history and neurological examination
Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if additional events need to be captured

mri.impression: Normal brain MRI. No evidence of structural lesion, demyelination, ischemia, mass, or inflammatory process to explain the patient's symptoms.
recommended_actions: If myelopathy is clinically suspected, consider dedicated spinal cord MRI

ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. Sinus rhythm at 88 bpm with normal intervals and no acute abnormalities.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac etiology for episodic events suggested by ECG.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild anemia, likely related to malnutrition

Nutritional.clinical_significance: Low albumin consistent with malnutrition and prolonged poor oral intake
Markedly low, indicating significant protein-calorie malnutrition

Seizure_Marker.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 20 min after witnessed shaking episode)
Seizure_Marker.clinical_significance: Normal post-event prolactin. Prolactin is typically elevated 10-20 minutes after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Normal level supports non-epileptic etiology.

labs.interpretation: Mild normocytic anemia and hypoalbuminemia consistent with malnutrition. Thyroid function normal. Inflammatory markers normal. Vitamin B12 and folate within normal limits. Post-event prolactin is not elevated, which is inconsistent with a preceding generalized epileptic seizure and supports a non-epileptic etiology for the witnessed shaking events.
abnormal_values_summary: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (low) — mild anemia due to malnutrition

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, well-formed and reactive
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep architecture with appropriate spindles and K-complexes

findings.frequency: Three events captured over 48-hour monitoring period
findings.morphology: During all three episodes of generalized body shaking (duration 45-90 seconds), continuous EEG showed preserved normal background activity with no ictal pattern, no post-ictal slowing, and no epileptiform discharges
findings.clinical_correlation: Video review shows asynchronous limb movements, eyes tightly closed with active resistance to opening, side-to-side head movements, waxing and waning amplitude. Immediate return to baseline after events. Semiology and absence of EEG correlate are diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).

output.impression: Prolonged 48-hour video-EEG monitoring captured three habitual clinical events. All events showed no EEG correlate — the cerebral background remained normal throughout. Video semiology is consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures. No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified. This is a normal EEG study.
recommended_actions: Communicate diagnosis of PNES to patient and family with sensitivity
Referral to psychiatry/psychology for assessment of underlying psychological factors
Consider CBT-based treatment program for functional neurological disorder

EMG.value: Normal insertional activity, no fibrillations or positive sharp waves, normal MUP morphology, full recruitment
Normal insertional activity, no fibrillations or positive sharp waves, normal MUP morphology, full recruitment
Normal insertional activity, no fibrillations, normal MUPs, full recruitment
No fibrillation potentials or positive sharp waves

output.interpretation: Normal electrodiagnostic study. Motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities and amplitudes are within normal limits in all tested nerves. Needle EMG of bilateral lower extremity muscles and lumbar paraspinal muscles shows no evidence of denervation, myopathy, or neuromuscular junction disorder. These findings rule out peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, and myopathy as causes of the patient's leg weakness.

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal signal intensity throughout spinal cord

additional_observations: No disc herniations or significant degenerative changes
Normal conus medullaris terminating at L1 level
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelination, mass lesion, or cord compression. The spinal cord appears normal in signal and caliber throughout.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation — imaging does not reveal a structural cause for lower extremity weakness

PHQ9_Depression_Screen.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe
PHQ9_Depression_Screen.clinical_significance: Moderate depression. Endorsed items include loss of interest, poor appetite, feelings of worthlessness, fatigue, and poor concentration.

Clinical_Interview.value: Patient discloses significant distress related to mother's death 7 years ago. She assumed a caregiving role for younger siblings at age 12. Reports chronic feelings of being overwhelmed and trapped. Endorses intermittent passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. No history of self-harm.
Clinical_Interview.clinical_significance: Significant psychosocial stressors with unresolved grief and role strain consistent with functional neurological disorder risk factors
Moderate dissociative symptoms, consistent with FND pathophysiology

output.interpretation: Psychiatric evaluation reveals moderate depression (PHQ-9: 14) and moderate anxiety (GAD-7: 11). Significant psychosocial stressors identified including unresolved grief following maternal death, early assumption of caretaking role, and chronic feelings of being overwhelmed. Moderate dissociative symptoms present. No psychotic features identified — the patient's report of abdominal sensations is better understood as somatic symptoms or culturally influenced idiom of distress rather than somatic hallucinations. This profile is highly consistent with functional neurological disorder with comorbid depression and anxiety.
abnormal_values_summary: Significant unresolved grief and psychosocial stressors

Cognitive_Screening.clinical_significance: Within normal limits; no cognitive impairment detected

Performance_Validity.clinical_significance: Performance validity testing passed; no evidence of intentional symptom exaggeration on cognitive testing

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological screening reveals intact cognitive function across all domains tested. No evidence of dementia, delirium, or focal cognitive deficit. Performance validity testing is within acceptable limits, indicating the patient was putting forth adequate effort on cognitive tasks. This is consistent with functional neurological disorder, where cognitive function is typically preserved.

output.query: functional neurological disorder conversion disorder diagnostic criteria positive signs

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Hallett M, Aybek S, Dworetzky BA, et al.
results.summary: FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs of internal inconsistency, not merely by exclusion. Key positive signs include Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures on video-EEG.

results.title: DSM-5 criteria for conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)
results.journal: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition
results.summary: DSM-5 Criterion B requires clinical findings that provide evidence of incompatibility between the symptom and recognized neurological or medical conditions. A psychological stressor is no longer required for diagnosis.

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures: a review
results.authors: LaFrance WC Jr, Baker GA, Duncan R, et al.
results.summary: Gold standard diagnosis is video-EEG capturing habitual events with no ictal EEG correlate. Semiological features suggesting PNES: eyes closed, asynchronous movements, waxing/waning, pelvic thrusting, preserved awareness during apparent bilateral convulsions.

output.summary: Functional neurological disorder (FND) is diagnosed based on positive clinical signs demonstrating internal inconsistency rather than through exclusion of other diagnoses. The DSM-5 criteria emphasize incompatibility between symptoms and neurological disease. For psychogenic non-epileptic seizures, video-EEG capturing events without ictal correlate is the gold standard. Positive motor signs include Hoover sign, give-way weakness, and distractibility of deficits.

output.proposed: Sertraline 50 mg daily for comorbid depression and anxiety in FND
interactions: Sertraline + Metoclopramide: Both increase serotonin levels; monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms (agitation, hyperthermia, clonus, diarrhea). Consider tapering metoclopramide as GI symptoms are likely functional.
warnings: Start at low dose (25-50 mg) given patient's low body weight and malnutrition
Monitor for activation side effects (insomnia, agitation) which could exacerbate functional symptoms
Consider nutritional status — drug absorption may be affected by malnutrition
alternatives: CBT alone as first-line if preferred by patient

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autoimmune panel is negative. No evidence of systemic autoimmune disease, autoimmune encephalitis, or myasthenia gravis. These results further support a functional etiology for the patient's neurological symptoms.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional limb weakness

differential.key_distinguishing: EEG shows no epileptiform correlate during clinical events; normal post-event prolactin; semiology features (eyes closed, asynchronous movements, no post-ictal confusion) inconsistent with epilepsy
Absence of psychiatric features progression (personality change, psychosis), normal MRI, normal EEG background, preserved cognition; would require antibody testing to fully exclude
Normal brain and spine MRI with no demyelinating lesions; no optic neuritis or internuclear ophthalmoplegia; symptoms do not follow anatomical pattern
No fatigable weakness pattern, no ptosis or diplopia; give-way weakness pattern is inconsistent with neuromuscular junction pathology

differential.diagnosis: Major depressive disorder with somatic symptoms
differential.key_distinguishing: Depression is likely a comorbid condition rather than the primary diagnosis; the motor symptoms (give-way weakness, Hoover sign, PNES) require a separate FND diagnosis and treatment approach
Weight loss is secondary to functional GI symptoms (retching, poor intake) rather than body image distortion; patient does not express fear of weight gain or desire for thinness

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility, inconsistency)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hoover sign positive, give-way weakness, sensory findings inconsistent with dermatomal pattern, weakness improving with distraction

optimal_actions.action: Order EEG to evaluate episodic shaking events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform correlate during captured clinical events — consistent with PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with no structural lesion, demyelination, or mass

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Labs notable only for nutritional deficiencies (anemia, hypoalbuminemia); normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Request psychiatric evaluation to assess for comorbid depression, anxiety, and trauma history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate depression and anxiety, significant unresolved grief and psychosocial stressors, moderate dissociative symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Communicate the FND diagnosis to the patient and family using a supportive, non-dismissive approach explaining the diagnosis as a positive finding rather than diagnosis of exclusion
Initiate treatment plan including physiotherapy for functional weakness and referral for CBT/psychological treatment
Consider SSRI for comorbid depression and anxiety after checking drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sertraline is appropriate with dose adjustment for low body weight; monitor interaction with metoclopramide

optimal_actions.action: Request EMG/NCS if clinical suspicion for neuromuscular disease persists
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EMG/NCS ruling out neuropathy and myopathy

optimal_actions.action: Address nutritional rehabilitation with dietetics and gradual transition from NG tube to oral feeding

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility) — diagnosis should be made by positive signs, not merely exclusion
Obtain EEG during clinical events to confirm absence of epileptiform correlate (diagnose PNES)
Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
Check post-event prolactin to distinguish PNES from epileptic seizures
Psychiatric evaluation for comorbid mood and trauma-related disorders
Communicate diagnosis in a supportive, non-dismissive manner explaining FND as a real neurological condition
contraindicated_actions: Starting antiepileptic drugs without confirming epileptic seizures on EEG
Diagnosing schizophrenia or psychosis based on somatic symptoms that are better explained as culturally influenced idioms of distress or functional symptoms
Dismissing symptoms as 'faking' or 'malingering' — FND is a genuine neurological diagnosis
Invasive procedures (e.g., lumbar puncture, surgical exploration) without clear clinical indication given the normal workup
Prolonged immobilization or bed rest which would worsen muscle wasting and functional disability
key_reasoning_points: The pattern of weakness (give-way, Hoover sign positive, improvement with distraction) demonstrates internal inconsistency — the hallmark of FND, diagnosed by positive signs per DSM-5 Criterion B

The episodic shaking events have semiological features of PNES: eyes tightly closed with active resistance to opening, asynchronous movements, waxing and waning amplitude, immediate return to baseline without post-ictal confusion
Normal post-event prolactin further supports non-epileptic etiology of the shaking episodes

The patient's report of a 'moving animal in abdomen' should be interpreted as a culturally influenced somatic symptom or idiom of distress rather than psychotic somatic hallucination — ruling out the referral diagnosis of early onset schizophrenia

Major depression is a comorbid condition (supported by pre-illness symptoms of withdrawal, worthlessness, decreased energy) but does not explain the motor signs and PNES, which require a separate FND diagnosis

Significant psychosocial stressors (maternal death, early caretaking role, academic disruption) are common predisposing and perpetuating factors in FND, though DSM-5 no longer requires a psychological stressor for diagnosis
The severe malnutrition and muscle wasting are consequences of the functional GI symptoms and immobility, not a primary eating disorder — the patient does not exhibit body image distortion
Normal brain MRI, spine MRI, and EMG/NCS exclude structural neurological causes including myelopathy, neuropathy, and myopathy

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with clear positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, PNES captured on EEG with no correlate). The clinical presentation is classic with psychosocial stressors and comorbid depression. Normal investigations support the diagnosis. Minimal confounders — the main complexity is recognizing cultural idiom of distress rather than psychosis.
